name: ConvertTo-Csv

options.name: -Delimiter
options.description: Specifies the delimiter to separate the property values in CSV strings. The default is a comma (`,`). Enter a character, such as a colon (`:`). To specify a semicolon (`;`) enclose it in single quotation marks.

name: -IncludeTypeInformation
-ITI
options.description: When this parameter is used the first line of the output contains `#TYPE` followed by the fully qualified name of the object type. For example, `#TYPE System.Diagnostics.Process`.

options.name: -InputObject
options.description: Specifies the objects that are converted to CSV strings. Enter a variable that contains the objects or type a command or expression that gets the objects. You can also pipe objects to `ConvertTo-CSV`.

name: -NoTypeInformation
-NTI
options.description: Removes the `#TYPE` information header from the output. This parameter became the default in PowerShell 6.0 and is included for backwards compatibility.

name: -QuoteFields
-QF
options.description: Specifies the names of the columns that should be quoted. When this parameter is used only the specified columns are quoted. This parameter was added in PowerShell 7.0.

options.name: -UseCulture
options.description: Uses the list separator for the current culture as the item delimiter. To find the list separator for a culture, use the following command: `(Get-Culture).TextInfo.ListSeparator`.

name: -UseQuotes
-UQ
options.description: Specifies when quotes are used in the CSV files. Possible values are:

description: Converts .NET objects into a series of character-separated value (CSV) strings.
